nativeRange.habitat: Found over rocky and coral reef habitats, generally between 3–30 m depth though recorded down to 275 m; adults sometimes school over adjacent sand and seagrass flats.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices for shelter
robust rockwork that the fish cannot easily rearrange
no fragile decor or unsecured equipment

diet.notes: Requires a varied meaty diet to mirror its natural feeding on benthic invertebrates. Mussels, clams, shrimp, krill, squid, and chopped fish are commonly recommended several times per day. Hard-shelled foods (whole crustaceans, snail shells) are generally advised to help wear down the continuously growing teeth and reduce the risk of dental overgrowth.

summary: A large, brightly patterned Atlantic and Caribbean triggerfish prized for its striking colors and bold personality. Generally considered unsafe for reef systems and known to be aggressive — best kept singly as the centerpiece of a spacious fish-only or FOWLR tank.
careNotes:
The Queen Triggerfish (Balistes vetula) is a large, intelligent, and notoriously bold Atlantic triggerfish that has been reported to reach up to about 60 cm (24 in) in total length, though most captive specimens settle in around 30–40 cm. Lifespans in well-kept aquariums tend to be 7–15 years; wild individuals have been documented surviving over 20 years.

A spacious system is generally advised — most experienced sources recommend roughly 500 US gallons (~1900 L) for a full-grown adult, with a tank footprint that emphasizes length and open swimming space. AquaMate biases the published 'minimum' to 1500 L as a cautious nice-round value reflecting both the fish's adult size and its very active, territorial lifestyle; smaller volumes (~300 gal / 1100 L) are sometimes kept successfully but tend to amplify aggression and stunt long-term growth.

Water chemistry is standard tropical reef: temperature around 22.5–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. The species is hardy and generally tolerant of minor parameter swings once acclimated.

Queen Triggerfish are not considered reef-safe. They will readily consume crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, lobsters), molluscs, sea urchins, starfish, and may nip or destroy corals and sessile invertebrates, both as targeted prey and incidentally through their habit of biting and rearranging tank decor.

Temperament is firmly aggressive. They have been known to redecorate aquascapes, attack heaters, powerheads, and airline tubing, and bite hands and equipment during maintenance — particularly as they mature. They should be added LAST to a tank and generally kept as the smallest or among the smallest of their tankmates so they don't dominate other large fish. Tankmates should be robust and similarly assertive: large angelfish, moray eels, large wrasses, and surgeonfish of similar size are commonly compatible. Avoid mixing with small, peaceful, or slow fish, with other triggerfish, and with crustaceans.

Diet is carnivorous and should be varied and protein-rich, with regular inclusion of hard-shelled foods such as whole shrimp, clams in shell, mussels, and similar items to wear down the continuously growing teeth — overgrown teeth can prevent feeding and become a husbandry emergency.

The species possesses a robust dorsal-fin 'trigger' mechanism that locks the first spine erect, and powerful, beak-like jaws capable of inflicting painful bites; care should be taken during handling, netting, and any hands-in-tank work. The species is not venomous in the strict sense, and its skin/flesh is not known to be toxic to other tank inhabitants (though wild specimens occasionally accumulate ciguatera toxin and are not safe for human consumption from some regions).

IUCN status is Near Threatened (assessment dated 2011); most aquarium-trade specimens are wild-caught from the Caribbean.

Cross-source notes — Adult size: LiveAquaria states 24 in (60 cm), FishBase 60 cm TL, AquariumSource 9–12 in typical with 24 in max, AquaticCommunity 60 cm. Tank size: LiveAquaria and AquariumSource both 500 gal (~1900 L); some hobbyists report 200–300 gal. AquaMate bias-larger pick: 1500 L. Temperature: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22–26°C), AquaticCommunity 74–79°F (23–26°C), FishBase 22.5–28°C — averaged to 22.5–26°C. Care level: LiveAquaria 'easy', but rated intermediate here to reflect tank-size, aggression, and dental-maintenance demands.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented. In the wild, Queen Triggerfish are demersal spawners — the female digs a shallow nest in sand and aggressively guards the eggs until hatching. Reproduction in aquaria is considered highly unlikely given the tank volumes, pairing dynamics, and feeding requirements involved.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species detail page (entered from the Triggerfish collection root). Provided minimum tank (500 gal), care level (easy), temperament (aggressive), reef compatibility (no), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), maximum size (24 in), diet, and family. Family root URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/triggerfish supplied by manifest.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Linnaeus, 1758), taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), max length 60 cm TL, wild temperature range 22.5–28°C, depth 2–275 m (usual 3–30 m), native Western and Eastern Atlantic distribution, trophic level 3.8 (benthic-invertebrate feeder), maximum age ~23 years, and IUCN status Near Threatened.

additional.name: AquariumSource
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (500 gal recommended; some hobbyists keep in 200–300 gal), water parameters (matching LiveAquaria), lifespan (7 yr avg, up to 13 yr in captivity), diet (mussels, squid, krill, shrimp, fish, sea urchins; hard-shell foods for dental health), reef compatibility (no), and temperament. Designated secondaries Reef2Reef wiki (403) and Fishkeeping World (404) failed; AquariumSource used as additional secondary.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 60 cm, temperature 74–79°F (23–26°C), aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (squid, shrimp, mussels, fish), and that conspecifics and small fish should be avoided.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive-breeding records found. Demersal nest spawner in the wild; tank-size and pair-bond requirements make hobby breeding effectively unreported.
